questions.question: Compare and contrast Kanban and Scrum with respect to change management, roles, and iteration structure. Under what project circumstances would you recommend Kanban over Scrum?
keyPoints: Scrum uses fixed-length sprints where change is batched until sprint boundary; Kanban allows continuous change at any point
Scrum prescribes three roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team); Kanban has no mandated roles but suggests service delivery focus
Iteration structure: Scrum commits to sprint backlog; Kanban flows work continuously without iteration boundaries

Recommendation scenarios: maintenance/support work, unpredictable priorities, regulatory compliance requiring audit trails, teams with high operational load, organizations transitioning from waterfall to agile

questions.question: Explain the concept of WIP (Work-In-Progress) limits in Kanban and how they contribute to improving flow efficiency. Include a mathematical relationship in your explanation.
keyPoints: WIP limits cap the number of items in any workflow stage simultaneously
Little's Law: Lead Time = WIP ÷ Throughput provides mathematical basis for prediction
Benefits: reveals bottlenecks, reduces context-switching, creates pull-based system, improves focus
Practical application: lower WIP in earlier stages prevents bottleneck formation downstream

questions.question: Describe the Crystal methodology family and explain how the selection of Crystal variant depends on team size and project criticality. Provide a specific scenario requiring Crystal Orange.
keyPoints: Crystal family includes Clear (1-8), Yellow (8-20), Orange (20-50), Red (50-300) scaled by team size
Criticality determines rigor: higher criticality requires more communication frequency and process discipline
Crystal Orange scenario: medium-sized team (30 developers) building enterprise resource planning system requiring 99.9% uptime—balances communication overhead with delivery speed

questions.question: Explain the five core activities of Feature-Driven Development (FDD) and discuss how the Chief Programmer role contributes to scalability in large projects.
keyPoints: FDD activities: Develop Overall Model → Build Feature List → Plan by Feature → Design by Feature → Build by Feature
Chief Programmer provides technical authority, code ownership, and coordination across feature teams
This role enables scaling by establishing clear technical leadership parallel to domain expertise, reducing coordination overhead typical in egalitarian team structures

questions.question: Discuss the three fundamental principles of Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM) and explain how its approach to scope management differs from Scrum's approach.
keyPoints: DSDM principles: Active User Involvement, Iterative and Incremental Development, Deliver on Time
Scope management: DSDM adjusts scope to meet fixed deadlines; Scrum extends time to meet scope
MoSCoW prioritization ensures critical 60% 'Must Haves' are delivered; lower-priority items can be deferred
Timeboxing in DSDM is more rigid than Scrum's sprint timeboxing—deadline never extends

questions.question: A software organization is selecting an agile methodology for three different projects: (a) a startup MVP with 4 developers needing rapid iteration, (b) a banking system with 60 developers requiring high reliability, and (c) an IT operations team handling 20+ daily support requests with unpredictable priorities. Recommend appropriate methodologies for each and justify with specific framework characteristics.
keyPoints: Startup MVP (4 developers): Crystal Clear—small team size, low criticality, emphasis on face-to-face communication, minimal documentation, rapid delivery matches startup needs
Banking system (60 developers): Crystal Red or hybrid Scrum at scale—high criticality (financial transactions), large team requires coordination mechanisms, need for formal processes and audit trails
Operations support: Kanban—continuous flow, WIP limits handle interrupt-driven work, ability to reprioritize instantly, no sprint commitments conflicting with operational demands
Justification must reference each framework's core principles and match to project characteristics including team size, criticality, predictability, and delivery cadence

questions.question: Explain Lean Software Development's seven principles and demonstrate how the principle 'Decide as Late as Possible' reduces project risk. Additionally, calculate the cost impact of a requirements change discovered in different phases using the exponential cost curve model (assume 10x cost increase per phase).
keyPoints: Seven principles: Eliminate Waste, Amplify Learning, Decide as Late as Possible, Deliver as Fast as Possible, Empower the Team, Build Integrity In, See the Whole
Decide as Late as Possible: later decisions incorporate more information, reduce commitment to potentially obsolete assumptions, allow market/customer feedback integration
Cost curve calculation: If change cost in requirements phase = $1, then: Requirements=$1, Design=$10, Development=$100, Testing=$1000, Deployment=$10,000
Example: A $50,000 feature change discovered in requirements vs. during testing represents $50,000 vs. $500,000 cost—10x difference demonstrates risk reduction
This principle directly reduces financial risk by deferring commitments until information is most stable

questions.question: Design a transition plan for a team moving from Scrum to Scrumban. Include specific recommendations for introducing WIP limits, preserving useful Scrum ceremonies, and measuring success. The team currently has 8 members with a 2-week sprint cycle and averages 15 story points per sprint.
keyPoints: Transition phases: 1) Visualize current workflow 2) Calculate current WIP from sprint data 3) Introduce WIP limits at 80% of current average 4) Add Kanban metrics tracking
Useful ceremonies to preserve: Daily standup (still valuable for communication), Sprint retrospective (reflection essential), Sprint planning (capacity planning useful)
Modifications: Remove sprint commitment rigidity, allow scope adjustment within sprint, add WIP limit focus in standups
Success metrics: Lead time reduction, throughput stability, WIP consistency, team velocity stability, bottleneck identification frequency
Scrumban allows evolution: teams can gradually adopt Kanban practices while retaining Scrum structure—flexibility is key
